content:
5 min read
Just now

--

When I started my internship at UNIKWORK, I knew the basics of React, JavaScript, Next.js, and Tailwind CSS from my coursework and self-learning — but I quickly realized that “knowing” a technology and actually building something real with it are two very different things. This one-month internship turned out to be one of the most valuable learning experiences I’ve had so far, and I want to share what I worked on, what I struggled with, and what I took away from it.
What I Worked On
My main task during the internship was building a user authentication project using React. This meant handling things like:

• Designing and building the login/signup UI components

• Managing form state and input validation

• Structuring components in a clean, reusable way

• Thinking through how authentication flows actually work in a real application, not just in a tutorial
It sounds simple on paper, but authentication is one of those features where small details matter a lot — how you handle errors, how you manage state across components, and how you make sure the user experience feels smooth even when something goes wrong.
Where I Struggled
I won’t pretend this was smooth sailing. I worked on this project solo, and there were a few specific topics in React and Next.js that genuinely challenged me — the kind of concepts that look straightforward in documentation but get confusing once you’re actually implementing them in a working project. I had to slow down, break the problem into smaller pieces, and really dig into why something wasn’t working instead of just copying a fix.
What made a real difference was having the support of my supervisor. Even though I was building the project independently, I could always go to them when I got stuck. Their guidance didn’t just help me fix the immediate problem — it helped me understand the reasoning behind the solution, which is a very different (and much more useful) kind of learning than just getting an answer.
What I Learned
If I had to sum up the biggest takeaways from this internship, they’d be:

• Technical growth — I got much more comfortable with React and Next.js beyond the surface level. Working through real struggles taught me more than passively completing tutorials ever could.

• Company culture — This was honestly one of the most eye-opening parts of the internship. Seeing how a real company operates day-to-day — how tasks are assigned, how communication happens, how work actually gets done — gave me a much clearer picture of professional life than any classroom setting could.

• Working through problems independently — Since I was working solo on the project, I learned how to research, debug, and problem-solve on my own, while also knowing when to ask for help instead of getting stuck for too long.
Final Thoughts
This one-month internship at UNIKWORK gave me a small but meaningful glimpse into what it’s like to work as a frontend developer in a real company setting. Building the user authentication project pushed me to actually apply React, JavaScript, Next.js, and Tailwind CSS in a practical context, and the challenges I faced along the way taught me just as much as the parts that went smoothly.
I’m walking away from this experience with stronger technical skills, a better understanding of workplace culture, and more confidence in tackling problems I don’t immediately know how to solve — which, if this internship taught me anything, is a skill I’ll need a lot more of going forward.What I Worked On
